text.title: eu-ai-act-compliance Core Rules
text.summary: Six testable rules covering classification, documentation, human gate, citation discipline, drift handling, and GDPR parallel track.

rule.statement: The pipeline MUST classify the AI system into one of the four EU AI Act risk tiers (unacceptable, high, limited, minimal) BEFORE any documentation is generated; classification determines the entire downstream documentation burden.
rule.rationale: Annex III enumerates the high-risk use cases. Misclassifying high-risk as limited exposes the organisation to fines of EUR 20M or 4% global turnover under Article 99.

rule.statement: No compliance document produced by the agent MAY be submitted to a regulator without explicit sign-off from a human with legal competence; the orchestrator MUST hard-stop at the review node.
rule.rationale: Conformity assessments and Article 11 technical documentation are legally binding. Submitting hallucinated or unreviewed content is a self-inflicted compliance breach.

rule.statement: All compliance artefacts (model cards, bias reports, data lineage, conformity assessments) MUST live in version-controlled storage with immutable history; ad-hoc files in shared drives are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Article 12 requires automatic logging and traceability across the system's lifecycle; without immutable history the audit trail fails.

rule.statement: Every Act-article citation in generated content MUST be validated against the official Act text (eur-lex.europa.eu) before the document leaves the pipeline; the reviewer subagent MUST flag any unverifiable citation.
rule.rationale: Agents confidently invent article numbers. A wrong citation surfaces in a regulator's review and destroys credibility.

rule.statement: The CI/CD pipeline MUST trigger re-classification when any feature, training-data scope, or deployment context changes; risk tier is not static.
rule.rationale: Substantial modification of a high-risk system requires a new conformity assessment under Article 43; missing the trigger is a compliance breach.

rule.statement: If the compliance pipeline itself processes personal data (e.g. bias checks on real user records), GDPR data-subject rights MUST be honoured independently of the Act track — DPIA, legal basis, retention limits all apply.
rule.rationale: GDPR and the AI Act are parallel regimes; one does not subsume the other.
